posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
2019 Château Thivin Côte de Brouilly - France, Burgundy, Beaujolais, Côte de Brouilly (12/5/2020)
My second sampling of the 2019 vintage, following Roilette Fleurie Tardive. This Thivin has some of the brooding, rustic, tannic character of that wine, though not nearly to the same degree. A medium-full bodied version, with plenty of dark, somewhat backward and brooding fruit, some violets, bit of anise, noticeable medium tannins, turning rocky and mineral on the finish. Despite a reputation as another warm, early vintage, following on the more juicy, lush, solar 17 and 18 vintages, this shows a lot of restraint, and a distinct need for some bottle age. A bit difficult to drink now, though not unenjoyable for someone with slightly masochistic tendencies, this shows great promise as a long term wine.

Night 2: a little more open 24 hours later, still a bit tannic, but showing more intensity and depth of fruit, excellent acidity, really quite tasty. This second glimpse is really reaffirming my initial sense, that this is going to be really good, but needs a few years in bottle. If you want to try one now, which won’t be a big mistake, I strongly suggest decanting for several hours at least, maybe double decant the day before. Raising my score on this for its future potential. (92pts.)

So glad to hear this, my wife & I got married in 2019 and so far it seems to have been a really good year in many key places. These age so well, I had a 1996 a few years ago & was immediately reminded of the “poor man’s Red Burgundy” comment I’ve heard a few times. Sounds like another year to stock up on this highly under-valued wine.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Thanks for the note. Is this the Les Sept Vignes?

No, just the regular, undesignated [Côte de!] Brouilly

Actually, it’s more or less the same cuvée: Kermit imports an unfiltered version of the wine that is sold as “Sept Vignes” in other markets, under the label “Côte de Brouilly” with no qualifiers.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
Thanks for the note. Is this the Les Sept Vignes?

No, just the regular, undesignated [Côte de!] Brouilly

Actually, it’s more or less the same cuvée: Kermit imports an unfiltered version of the wine that is sold as “Sept Vignes” in other markets, under the label “Côte de Brouilly” with no qualifiers.

Good info! This is indeed the Kermit bottling, so you’re saying it’s the same as Sept Vignes? How do the various cuvees differ?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
No, just the regular, undesignated [Côte de!] Brouilly

Actually, it’s more or less the same cuvée: Kermit imports an unfiltered version of the wine that is sold as “Sept Vignes” in other markets, under the label “Côte de Brouilly” with no qualifiers.

Good info! This is indeed the Kermit bottling, so you’re saying it’s the same as Sept Vignes? How do the various cuvees differ?

Simply that the Sept Vignes is filtered and for sold in the domaine’s other markets, whereas the Kermit version is unfiltered and doesn’t say “Sept Vignes”. When I visit, they tend to show the Kermit version for review. I would say that the Sept Vignes is a bit aromatically purer but a bit less rich and textural, but these are fine distinctions.

posts.name: J. D Antonio . j _ @ _ y
posts.cooked: Thanks for the clarification, Alan/William. We get the Les Sept Vignes label in our market. The straight Brouilly is labeled Reverdon, which, coincidently, I’m drinking the 2018 right now. A little lighter, more red-fruited, and straight-forward than the Cote de Brouilly Les Sept Vignes. It’s nice, but for $5/bottle more, I’d rather have the Les Sept Vignes.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Thanks for the clarification, Alan/William. We get the Les Sept Vignes label in our market. The straight Brouilly is labeled Reverdon, which, coincidently, I’m drinking the 2018 right now. A little lighter, more red-fruited, and straight-forward than the Cote de Brouilly Les Sept Vignes. It’s nice, but for $5/bottle more, I’d rather have the Les Sept Vignes.

All the Brouilly is labeled Reverdon, that’s the name of the cuvée. Alan has been talking about the Côte de Brouilly, even if he referred to it as the “regular Brouilly” on one occasion. In fairness to him, The Wine Advocate’s database did not include Côte de Brouilly as an appellation when I took over out Beaujolais coverage, despite it dating back to the 1930s—contemporaneous with the Brouilly AOC.

You get the Sept Vignes because you are not in the USA where Kermit Lynch is the exclusive importer and brings in the cuvée discussed in this thread.

posts.cooked:

A little lighter, more red-fruited, and straight-forward than the Cote de Brouilly Les Sept Vignes. It’s nice, but for $5/bottle more, I’d rather have the Les Sept Vignes.

All the Brouilly is labeled Reverdon, that’s the name of the cuvée. Alan has been talking about the Côte de Brouilly, even if he referred to it as the “regular Brouilly” on one occasion. In fairness to him, The Wine Advocate’s database did not include Côte de Brouilly as an appellation when I took over out Beaujolais coverage, despite it dating back to the 1930s—contemporaneous with the Brouilly AOC.

You get the Sept Vignes because you are not in the USA where Kermit Lynch is the exclusive importer and brings in the cuvée discussed in this thread.

That’s because I didn’t know there was a difference, so just used the shorthand, apparently mistakenly. The only bottlings I can remember seeing here in the States are the Cote de Brouilly, Reverdon, and Zaccharie.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Thanks for the clarification, Alan/William. We get the Les Sept Vignes label in our market. The straight Brouilly is labeled Reverdon, which, coincidently, I’m drinking the 2018 right now. A little lighter, more red-fruited, and straight-forward than the Cote de Brouilly Les Sept Vignes. It’s nice, but for $5/bottle more, I’d rather have the Les Sept Vignes.

All the Brouilly is labeled Reverdon, that’s the name of the cuvée. Alan has been talking about the Côte de Brouilly, even if he referred to it as the “regular Brouilly” on one occasion. In fairness to him, The Wine Advocate’s database did not include Côte de Brouilly as an appellation when I took over out Beaujolais coverage, despite it dating back to the 1930s—contemporaneous with the Brouilly AOC.

You get the Sept Vignes because you are not in the USA where Kermit Lynch is the exclusive importer and brings in the cuvée discussed in this thread.

That’s because I didn’t know there was a difference, so just used the shorthand, apparently mistakenly. The only bottlings I can remember seeing here in the States are the Cote de Brouilly, Reverdon, and Zaccharie.

Côte de Brouilly = higher altitude, on the slopes of an extinct volcano, lots of volcanic “blue stone” as they call it.

Brouilly = lower altitude, more heterogenous geologically, but often with quite a bit of clay.

The generalization is that the former makes more serious, age-worthy wines and the latter more fruit-driven wines, but quite a bit of that is likely due to how people choose to make them.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: A really great discussion on one of my favorite wine estates. I buy as much Thivin as probably any other wine in my stable. Hard to imagine a more consistently good-to-great winery from year to year. I don’t even think I have missed a vintage since 2006. The cuvees have become a bit confusing, so again, thanks here.

posts.cooked: I opened the '19 the other day and liked it a lot as well. I agree with Alan it’s slightly less foreboding than the '19 Roilette Tardive. I preferred the Tardive slightly in this vintage, but both are great.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
I opened the '19 the other day and liked it a lot as well. I agree with Alan it’s slightly less foreboding than the '19 Roilette Tardive. I preferred the Tardive slightly in this vintage, but both are great.

David, see my updated note from the next night, the Thivin showed more of itself after 24 hours. The Roilette, OTOH, didn’t really change or open the next day. Not exactly sure what that means, but I’m glad to have a stash of the Thivin.

posts.display_username: Patrick T a y l o r

posts.name: Marshall Manning
posts.cooked:
I visited Thivin about this time last year. The aroma of their cellar should be turned into cologne. Yeast, must, woody, stone…

Thivin was always one of the best stops on the Kermit Lynch tour. Not only are the wines consistently delicious, but Evelyne Geoffrey is an AMAZING cook, and would do a multi-course lunch for us that was better than many of the restaurants we ate in. She would finish with a few desserts, and if you were there on the right year, you’d get to try her famous butter pie, about as rich and decadent a dessert as you could imagine. I always felt sorry for Nicole Chanrion, which was always the next stop. Her wines are delicious, too, but everyone was so stuffed from the lunch at Thivin that they couldn’t eat her lovely gougeres and other snacks she provided.

posts.name: Patrick T a y l o r
posts.cooked:
I visited Thivin about this time last year. The aroma of their cellar should be turned into cologne. Yeast, must, woody, stone…

Thivin was always one of the best stops on the Kermit Lynch tour. Not only are the wines consistently delicious, but Evelyne Geoffray is an AMAZING cook, and would do a multi-course lunch for us that was better than many of the restaurants we ate in. She would finish with a few desserts, and if you were there on the right year, you’d get to try her famous butter pie, about as rich and decadent a dessert as you could imagine. I always felt sorry for Nicole Chanrion, which was always the next stop. Her wines are delicious, too, but everyone was so stuffed from the lunch at Thivin that they couldn’t eat her lovely gougeres and other snacks she provided.

We did not get lunch, but got to taste with Claude in the family dining room, complete with the dog and cat sleeping in front of the crackling fire.

Nicole was great, too. She opened a 2010 Brouilly which we loved. It was still at the original release price of 14 euros! I schlepped a couple bottles home.
